era_context: The mid-15th century marks a pivotal moment in world history: the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in the Balkans, European states are engaged in the final phases of the Hundred Years' War, the Italian Renaissance is flourishing, the Ming Dynasty is at its height in China, Portuguese maritime exploration is accelerating, and the Inca Empire is expanding in South America. This is an era of transition from medieval to early modern structures across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian and Steppe polities (sparse written records in European sources)
Pacific Islander societies (no contemporary written records)
Southeast Asian kingdoms (limited documentation in European sources)

events.title: Gutenberg begins printing press development
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg is developing the movable-type printing press in Mainz. By 1440, he is likely working on the key innovations that will lead to the printing press, including the use of metal type, oil-based ink, and the printing mechanism. The exact timeline of his innovations remains debated, but by the mid-1440s, he had likely perfected the technology. This innovation will revolutionise information dissemination across Europe, though the first dated printed work (the 42-line Bible) would not appear until circa 1455.

sources.name: Gutenberg's legal documents and contracts (Mainz archives)
Later accounts by Johann Trithemius and others
Historiographic consensus from printing history scholarship

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Gutenberg's innovations is uncertain. Documents place him in Mainz by 1444, but the development of the press likely occurred over several years (1440–1455). The 1440 date is conventional; the technology was probably not fully mature until the early 1450s.
cross_references: Led to 1455-001 (Printing of the Gutenberg Bible)

events.title: Eton College founded
events.description: King Henry VI of England founds Eton College, a prestigious school near Windsor. The college is established as a centre of learning and is intended to educate boys for service to the Church and the Crown. Eton College becomes one of the most prestigious educational institutions in England and remains influential in English education to the present day. The founding reflects the growing emphasis on education and humanist learning in the 15th century.
events.certainty_note: The founding of Eton College is well-documented in royal charters and college records. The date of 1440 is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader educational expansion in 15th-century England

events.title: King's College, Cambridge founded
events.description: King Henry VI of England founds King's College, Cambridge, as part of his broader educational patronage. The college is established to educate boys from Eton College and to provide a centre of learning at the University of Cambridge. King's College becomes one of the most prestigious colleges at Cambridge and remains influential in English education. The founding reflects the growing emphasis on education and humanist learning in the 15th century.
events.certainty_note: The founding of King's College, Cambridge is well-documented in royal charters and university records. The date of 1440 is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader educational expansion in 15th-century England

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1440, Portuguese caravels have reached the Senegal River and are establishing trading posts and forts. These expeditions are motivated by the search for gold, spices, and a sea route to Asia, as well as the conversion of African peoples to Christianity. The voyages mark the beginning of European colonial expansion in Africa.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is well-documented in royal records and contemporary chronicles. The specific achievements of 1440 are attested in Zurara's chronicle, though written after the events.
cross_references: Part of broader Age of Exploration (1415 onwards)

events.title: Inca expansion under Pachacuti
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, the ninth Inca emperor, continues his military campaigns to expand the Inca Empire. By 1440, he has already defeated the Chancas and is consolidating control over the Andean region. His reign marks the beginning of the Inca Empire's rapid expansion, which will eventually create the largest pre-Columbian state in the Americas. Pachacuti's military innovations and administrative reforms transform the Inca state into a centralised empire.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca oral tradition recorded by Spanish chroniclers)
Pedro Cieza de León, Chronicle of Peru
Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, Nueva Corónica y Buen Gobierno

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is notoriously uncertain, as the Inca did not use a written calendar system. The date of 1440 is assigned by modern scholars based on Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions recorded centuries after the events. The actual date could vary by several decades.
cross_references: Led to 1532-001 (Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire)

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidation
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to consolidate its power and expand its influence in East Asia. By 1440, the Ming state has established a sophisticated bureaucratic system based on Confucian principles and the examination system. The reign of the Xuande Emperor (1426–1435) and his successors has seen the stabilisation of Ming rule and the expansion of Chinese influence. The Ming state is at the height of its power and cultural achievement.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative structures and consolidation are well-documented in Ming records. The state of the Ming Dynasty in 1440 is attested in multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Gutenberg's invention of the printing press is often attributed solely to him, but woodblock printing and earlier movable-type systems existed in Asia, and some scholars argue that Gutenberg's specific innovation was the combination of technologies rather than the invention of printing itself. The founding of Eton College and King's College, Cambridge are sometimes presented as purely educational initiatives, but they were also instruments of royal power and control. Portuguese exploration is often presented as driven by noble ideals of discovery and conversion, but the primary motives were economic (gold, spices) and the beginning of the slave trade. Pachacuti's dates are highly uncertain; some scholars place his reign earlier or later, and the extent of his conquests by 1440 is debated. The Ming Dynasty's consolidation is sometimes presented as a period of stability, but internal conflicts and the beginning of the shift toward isolationism were already underway.
historiographic_note: The year 1440 is well-documented in European sources (English, Portuguese, papal records) but less thoroughly in Asian sources available to Western scholarship. The founding of Eton College and King's College, Cambridge are extensively documented in royal charters and institutional records. Portuguese exploration is documented in royal records and contemporary chronicles, but African perspectives are almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources. Chinese records are rich but often filtered through later compilations. The historiography of this period is heavily shaped by European state records; the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups are largely invisible. Inca chronology is particularly problematic, as the Inca did not use a written calendar system, and Spanish colonial sources are often contradictory.

graph_edges.note: Gutenberg's development of the printing press in 1440 led to the printing of the Gutenberg Bible circa 1455.
The founding of Eton College and King's College, Cambridge were part of the same educational patronage initiative by Henry VI.
Portuguese exploration of the African coast in 1440 led to the beginning of the Portuguese slave trade in 1441.
Pachacuti's expansion of the Inca Empire in 1440 created the state that would be conquered by Spanish conquistadors in 1532.
Ming consolidation in 1440 was disrupted by the Tumu Crisis in 1449.
